title: How to Make People Like You in 90 Seconds

chapters.title: Connection through mirroring
chapters.text:
Ever notice how close friends start finishing each other's sentences? Or how you unconsciously match your walking pace with someone you like? That's not a coincidence; it's synchronization, your brain's way of saying, "We're on the same team." And here's the exciting part: you can trigger this deliberately.

Synchronization is an essential communication element that helps maintain old connections and acquire new ones. What's interesting is that similar life experiences, like a shared hometown, a mutual struggle, or even the same frustration with a delayed flight, bind people more powerfully than professional achievements or social status ever could.

Partners in happy couples demonstrate this phenomenon perfectly. They begin to adopt each other's habits, gestures, and phrases after they've spent a few years together. This behavior only strengthens their attachments to each other. You can create this same effect with others by using deliberate mirroring.

Mirroring usually occurs when trying to align attitude, body language, specific gestures, tone of voice, certain words, and even breathing with another person. Hand and arm motions are the easiest to mirror, but you can also match body posture or position. In your next conversation, subtly match the other person's posture. If they lean back, you lean back — a few seconds later, not immediately. If they're speaking slowly and softly, bring your own pace down. If they gesture with their hands, let your hands do the same. The key is subtlety. Remember that you're harmonizing, not mimicking.

Mirroring works verbally, too. Open questions are reliable icebreakers: Who? When? What? Why? Where? How? These invite opinions and feelings. Instead of "Did you like the conference?" (yes or no), try "What did you think of it?" or "How did you end up in this field?" Combining them with active listening and feedback in the form of nods or a simple "uh-huh" can encourage a person to reveal a lot of new information. Here's a powerful combination: ask an open question, lean in slightly, and when they answer, nod and say, "Tell me more about that." Watch how they open up.

Most people are waiting to be heard. Be the one who actually listens.

However, mirroring is not about faking a personality but building a safe and comfortable bridge between two strangers. When the connection deepens, something interesting happens: they start mirroring you back, often without realizing it. The closer you get, the more naturally synchronization flows both ways.

So, you've got body language and mirroring down. Now let's add one more layer: tuning into how people process the world — through sight, sound, or feeling.

chapters.title: You can't make a first impression twice
chapters.text:
Think of your favorite movie characters and how the producers introduced them. Most likely, they were powerful presentations you will remember for a long time. Iron Man, James Bond, and Daenerys Targaryen captured viewers' attention in their first episodes and held it for years. Now, real life may not come with dragons, explosions, or a dramatic soundtrack… but the truth is, you can create a first impression that's just as memorable. There is a whole strategy for it.

The first three or four seconds of a new acquaintance are precious because, during them, the brain subconsciously decides whether you will feel safe and comfortable to be around. Use them to the fullest by applying the Open-Beam-Hi!-Lean scheme. Here's how it works:

1. Open your heart and body. Crossed arms or legs make people think you are not ready to be honest or talk, which may be far from the truth. Before you approach anyone, do a quick body check: uncross your arms, relax your shoulders, and turn your torso toward the person. Think "open chest, open conversation."
2. Eye and Beam. Eye contact and a friendly smile can instantly win a person's sympathy, so don't be overly serious. The key is to smile before you speak — let your face arrive first. Make eye contact, hold it for a beat, then smile. That sequence signals warmth before you've said a single word.
3. Say: Hi! After the visual, it's time for the verbal. Repeat the other person's name and introduce yourself. This way, you will remember them better and show interest in them. "Nice to meet you, Michael. I'm Sarah," sounds better than just a name.
4. Lean. Want to show affection without words? Nod slightly or lean forward. Most people do this unconsciously when listening to something interesting, but you can use this tip purposefully. A subtle forward lean of just a few inches signals "I'm engaged." But keep it natural. You're leaning in, not lunging.

These tricks make the conversation a solid foundation for a long-lasting relationship, so don't hesitate to try them. The more you practice, the more automatic it becomes.

Charm is just attention, generously given.

chapters.title: Negotiating the maze of emotions
chapters.text:
If the first meeting is the foundation of communication, emotions are the bricks that build everything else. Sometimes, they work even better than words; an angry expression, tears in the eyes, or a knowing smile often communicate more than any sentence could. The human brain is wired to respond to emotional signals. We mirror positive emotions and react to negative ones almost automatically. You can use this plasticity to build deeper connections.

Positive emotions are usually delightful and create an atmosphere of cooperation. Imagine there is a gloomy employee at one checkout and a smiling one at the other. Which line do you join? You already know the answer.

Here's what's happening beneath the surface: when someone treats you warmly, your brain releases oxytocin — the trust hormone. You literally become more inclined to help them, work with them, and seek them out again. So, try being friendly if you're attending an important business meeting. Even if it doesn't bring immediate results, a positive impression can pay off in the future.

What should you do when someone responds to your warmth with an unexpected emotion? Imagine you joked about cats ruling the world but didn't know that your business partner's pet died yesterday, and now you're facing sudden sadness. Remember that communication is a flexible process, so use the following framework to save the moment:

• Firstly, identify what you actually want from this conversation: is it to close a deal, build a relationship, or simply leave a good impression?
• Then get feedback from their face, tone, and body. What just shifted?
• Finally, adjust your response to meet them where they are, not where you expected them to be.

In the cat joke scenario, acknowledge immediately: 'I'm so sorry, I had no idea.' If you've shared a similar loss, briefly mention it, then pause and let them lead. That moment of genuine empathy? It often creates a stronger bond than if the awkwardness had never happened.

Looking for common ground or experience in a conversation is the best way to get along.

Negative emotions are not bad; they can bring people together even better than positive ones, but are more challenging to work with.

Did you know? Young diplomats and spies in many countries take emotional intelligence courses as an indispensable part of conflict resolution.

You're learning to read the room. Now let's talk about what your body is saying when you're not even speaking because people are reading you just as carefully.

chapters.title: Your gestures are your best allies
chapters.text:
Have you ever admired the beauty of dance? Although dancers don't say a word, their skillful movements fascinate and tell a whole story, leaving no one indifferent. Your body speaks too, constantly, and the people around you are listening, whether you realize it or not.

Ancient orators like Demosthenes and Cicero recognized this and developed strategies for using gestures in their speeches. Generations of masters perfected them, and you can use their most practical insights today.

Imagine you and the person you're talking to have large heart signs on your chests, connected by a fragile string. If you cross your arms, you will break them because closed body language and quick gestures are characteristic of people in defensive positions.

The people you talk to also have emotions and can be just as nervous as you.

On the other hand, when you relax, gesture slowly, and have an open posture, you create a powerful impression of a confident person. This knowledge can also help you read how ready someone else is for connection: watch for crossed arms, an angled-away body, or fidgeting as signals that they're not quite there yet.

The better you are at connecting with other people, the better the quality of your life. ~ Nicholas Boothman

Nicholas Boothman

And that connection starts with how you carry yourself. Facial expressions are equally important because people can lie, but their bodies can't. The Three "Vs" of communication reveal something crucial about how we actually receive messages:

• Visual: accounts for 55% of all incoming information
• Vocal: 38% of what we absorb comes from tone of voice
• Verbal: only 7% comes from the actual words

When your friend says "I'm fine" in a flat voice while staring at the floor, you don't believe them. You trust the 93% over the 7%. Everyone does.

Here's how to use this: before any important conversation, do a quick alignment check. Say your key point out loud: does your voice match? Your body? If you're saying "I'm excited" with hunched shoulders and a flat tone, you're sending the wrong message. So, keep all three Vs consistent.

If you want people to believe you, try to ensure that all three "Vs" are consistent, even if you are tired, having a bad day, or nervous. This awareness is a shield and a superpower, as it helps you recognize when someone's words and body don't match, and it lets you communicate more authentically yourself.

Do you want to succeed in love? Gestures and facial expressions will also help you flirt. Show your sympathy a bit more actively, for example, by holding your gaze a second longer or giving an extra smile. It's subtle, but it's unmistakable.

Now that you can read body language and align your own signals, let's explore how to create that feeling of "we just click" through the art of mirroring.

chapters.title: Connection through mirroring
chapters.text:

chapters.title: Play the senses like a piano
chapters.text:
Every communication begins with data collection. Your hearing, smell, vision, and skin collect information like special agents and then transmit it to the general command center — your brain. All of this happens in a split second, and now you have the base without which it is impossible to start a meaningful dialogue. However, not all agents are equally effective, so people perceive the same thing differently.

See differences as not a barrier but an opportunity to learn something new.

Depending on the type of information that is easiest to perceive, Richard Bandler and John Grinder, the founders of Neuro-Linguistic Programming, grouped people into three types:

• Visual — 55%
• Auditory — 15%
• Kinesthetic — 30%.

People usually mix all the senses to describe their experiences, but one sense usually dominates. Here's how to spot each type. Visual people say things like "I see what you mean" or "Let me paint you a picture." Auditory people favor "That sounds right" or "I hear you." Kinesthetic people go for "I feel like..." or "Let's touch base." Once you start listening for these cues, you'll hear them everywhere.

The best strategy to tune into someone's sensory wavelength is to start by using all three types. As the relationship develops, you'll find out which one is the most powerful.

Listening is the other side of the conversation coin. ~ Nicholas Boothman

Nicholas Boothman

Let's look at how to deal with each type. Imagine you're a jewelry salesperson who has three customers. The first of them is a beautifully dressed woman with a visual type of thinking. Invite her to look at the gems' sparkle and describe how these silver earrings will match her outfit. Use phrases like "I see what you mean," "Can you imagine?" or "You have an interesting vision of this situation."

The second client is a musician choosing a chain for his first big concert. He is an auditory type, so phrases like "Sounds familiar" or "Tell me more" can satisfy him, especially if you let him rummage through the products before choosing.

The third client is a man looking for an engagement ring for his girlfriend. He's kinesthetic, so let him touch the different metals and ask, "How do you feel about it?" to build rapport.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Here's the truth: most people go through life hoping connection will "just happen." They rely on luck, chemistry, or the right moment.
You don't have to.

You now have a real blueprint for building connections purposefully, and you know the four-second sequence that transforms first impressions. Furthermore, you know how to make first impressions land in seconds, as well as how to handle emotional shifts without panicking. But knowing isn't the same as doing. The gap between reading this and becoming genuinely better at connection is just one thing: practice.

Imagine six months from now: you walk into any room and feel genuinely curious to meet whoever's there instead of anxious about how you'll come across. That version of you starts with one conversation today.

Try this

• Take a VAK test online to determine whether you're a visual, auditory, or kinesthetic learner. Before you can understand others, you need to understand yourself.
• Stand in front of a mirror and imagine that you are speaking to a large audience on any topic. Determine the gestures and facial expressions you want to use and which are most successful.
• Work on your smile. Smile with your lips and the muscles around your eyes to make your emotions seem sincere.

Connection isn't a talent you either have or don't. It's a skill that improves with every practice. Start today.

Read it till the end?
